content.text: You join a debate club at 12 because your cousin, Jamie, said that’s how you get into college. You don’t care about college. You care that Jamie’s confident and people listen when she talks.
content.imageDescription: A 12-year-old sits nervously at a school debate club meeting. Jamie, an older cousin, stands confidently nearby, talking animatedly as other kids listen to her. The focus is on the main character glancing at Jamie, admiration clear on their face.

content.text: The first debate is about social media. You memorize the opinion your team leader sends in the group chat. You recite it word for word. You win.
content.imageDescription: In a brightly lit classroom, the protagonist stands at a podium, reading from a sheet, while judges and students watch. Their expression is fixed in concentration. In the next moment, a small trophy is passed to them amid understated applause.

content.text: Your trophy looks plastic but it’s heavy, like maybe it matters. Jamie says you did great.
content.imageDescription: Close-up of the protagonist holding a shiny plastic trophy, looking at it with mixed emotions. Jamie leans in with a proud smile, giving the protagonist an approving thumbs-up.

content.text: You google how to win every argument. The first result says: sound certain.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist sits at a desk at home, intently typing on a laptop. The search result 'How to win every argument' is visible, highlighted at the top, with the phrase 'sound certain' clearly displayed on the screen.

content.text: You practice talking with zero doubt, even when you have all the doubt in the world. You like how easy it is to sound like you know things.
content.imageDescription: In their bedroom, the protagonist stands in front of a mirror, practicing speech with a confident, assertive posture, while their real facial expressions reveal underlying uncertainty.

content.text: You scroll forums full of bold text and stock images; someone posts a thread called “NASA’s Biggest Lie.” You click without even thinking. It’s a video: watercolor animation, British accent. The earth is flat and round is a scam. Gravity is a story. You watch it twice.
content.imageDescription: A computer screen glows in a dim room, displaying a forum page with sensational headlines. Over the protagonist’s shoulder, the thread 'NASA’s Biggest Lie' is clicked, and a video with animated scenes and captions about flat earth concepts plays.

content.text: There are links in the description, suggested readings, testimonials in all caps. People say their eyes are open now. You want your eyes open too.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist leans forward, entranced by the list of all-caps comments and glowing links on the screen. Their eyes are wide, reflecting curiosity and a wish to understand more.

content.text: You tell Jamie. She laughs. You laugh too but google “is the earth flat” when you get home.
content.imageDescription: At a kitchen table, the protagonist tells Jamie something earnestly. Jamie laughs heartily; the protagonist forces a laugh, eyes downcast. Later, in their room, the protagonist searches 'is the earth flat' online in private.

content.text: Endless pages. Algorithms nudge you along. Dots connect: Antarctica is a wall, pilots are in on it, look how airplanes actually fly. Ten minutes turns into two hours, then three. You don’t notice the sun set.
content.imageDescription: Multiple browser tabs open on a laptop, filled with conspiracy diagrams and maps. The room grows dark as the daylight shifts to evening, the protagonist totally absorbed, unaware of the changing light outside their window.

content.text: In class, someone asks what you’re reading on your phone. You say, “Did you know photos from space are just CGI?” It comes out smooth. The sentence sounds like someone else’s voice, but no one stops you. You repeat it at lunch. It gets laughs. You’re confident anyway.
content.imageDescription: In a busy classroom, a student peers at the protagonist’s phone. The protagonist delivers their new fact with practiced confidence. In the cafeteria, they repeat the claim, earning amused laughter from a table of students.

content.text: Someone shares a meme: globe defenders eating fake food. You laugh, share it in a group chat. Two people reply with yeses, one with a laughing emoji.
content.imageDescription: A phone screen shows a colorful meme ridiculing 'globe defenders.' The protagonist laughs, forwarding the image to a buzzing group chat. Notifications pop up: 'yes,' 'yes,' and a laughing face emoji in quick succession.

content.text: You start seeing the world differently—edges, corners, not curves. Maps look suspicious. The globe in your science classroom seems vaguely threatening.
content.imageDescription: Camera pans over maps and a classroom globe. The protagonist gazes at them with suspicion, as if noticing new details. The globe sits on a shelf, casting a looming shadow over the protagonist.

content.text: Your browser history fills up with forums and YouTube channels that say GPS is just triangulation, and water doesn’t curve. You say these things out loud until they sound true.
content.imageDescription: A collage of browser tabs reveals video titles and forums about GPS and water theories. The protagonist, alone at a kitchen table, repeats one of the lines out loud with increasing conviction.

content.text: Dinnertime with your mom: did you know there’s never been an actual photo of earth? She shakes her head, tells you to finish your peas. You do.
content.imageDescription: Dinner at home, the protagonist looks up from their plate to share a conspiratorial fact. Their mom sighs, unfazed, and gestures to finish eating. The protagonist pushes peas around their plate, uncertain.

content.text: Later you watch videos with titles like “10 Proofs NASA Lied” and “Pilots Expose The Truth.” You stop caring if the earth is flat or not. You care that you sound like you know something no one else knows. You care about being the one with secret knowledge.
content.imageDescription: Late at night, illuminated by a screen, the protagonist watches sensationalist videos. In the quiet room, a look of satisfaction grows as they jot down notes, savoring the feeling of knowing secrets.

content.text: Your friends stop arguing. They shrug, say it’s your thing now.
content.imageDescription: A group of friends chat during lunchtime. When the protagonist mentions their new beliefs, the others exchange resigned glances and dismissively wave it off as 'your thing.' The protagonist sits slightly apart, still engaged.

content.text: You start a TikTok. The first video, grainy, late at night: “What if I told you gravity is made up?” Ten people like it. A dozen comment with brain emojis and “WOKE.” Someone calls you a genius in DMs. You believe them, a little. You fall asleep scrolling through likes. Your dreams fill with horizons that never bend.
content.imageDescription: Close-up of the protagonist’s phone recording a low-light TikTok video: they speak excitedly into the camera. Followed by images of notifications and messages of praise. The scene transitions to the protagonist asleep, dreamlike horizons stretching behind them, flat and endless.

content.text: In earth science class, the teacher says, “Any questions about the sphere?” The room is silent. You raise your hand. You say, “How do you know?” The teacher goes into orbit, axial tilt, satellite images. You counter with a link you saved in notes. She sighs, asks you to trust the evidence. Evidence is what got everyone into this mess. You bring up ice walls and faked images. The rest of the class looks uncomfortable, eyes dart to the clock. You like the attention. You like the feeling of certainty.
content.imageDescription: A tense classroom scene: the teacher at the front points to a diagram of a globe. The protagonist confidently challenges her, referencing notes on a phone. Other students exchange uneasy glances or look at the clock while the protagonist soaks up the spotlight.

content.text: Jamie stops texting as much. You find new people who agree, all online, all anonymous, all sharing proof in recycled memes and untraceable citations. Your certainty grows — calibrated by repetition, boosted by the next post that says what you want to hear.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist checks their phone, noticing a lack of messages from Jamie. Then, multiple chat bubbles pop up on-screen with usernames and avatars, sharing memes and dubious links, the protagonist nodding along enthusiastically.

content.text: You don’t test the theory. You don’t buy a ticket to see the curve of the earth. You can’t afford a weather balloon. You join arguments under articles, post the same sources you were handed, watch the same talking heads who speak in circles but always land on, “Open your mind.” Your mind is open. So open things fall out.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist scrolls past images of hot-air balloons and travel ads, looking wistfully but never acting. Instead, they argue in online comment sections, copying links, watching pundits on screen, absorbing more catchphrases.

content.text: Elections approach. You don’t care who wins as long as they admit the truth about satellites. You stop going to debate club. Too many rules.
content.imageDescription: Posters for an upcoming election line the school hallway. The protagonist ignores them, walking past the debate club meeting room, pausing at the door but turning away, their club badge tucked in a pocket.

content.text: Instead, you make slideshows — one with a headline, one with the picture of a cat in a satellite helmet, one with a blinking gif that says “Question Everything.” You post them everywhere. Most people ignore you. Some people comment with angry paragraphs. You screenshot these and send them to your group chat. You get validation every time someone calls you crazy.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist assembles eye-catching slides on their laptop: headlines, funny cat images, and a blinking “Question Everything” gif. They post online, scrolling through responses—mostly ignored, but some angry replies are screenshotted. The protagonist smirks at the notifications.

content.text: Your mom starts getting emails from school. Worried teachers. Too many conspiracy papers for class assignments. She sits you down. “Do you believe the earth is flat?” You smile. You shrug. You tell her: “I know things other people are afraid to.” She sighs. She’s tired. She says maybe take a break from the internet. You nod. You don’t. Your phone is always nearby. Your notifications keep coming. You need to know what people think of what you think. You need someone telling you you’re right. It matters more than being right.
content.imageDescription: A worried mother reads an email on her laptop at the kitchen table, then faces the protagonist for a serious talk. The protagonist shrugs with a forced smile. Later, the protagonist secretly checks their phone behind their schoolbooks, obsessed with incoming notifications.

content.text: You go to a party one night. Someone hands you a drink. The conversation turns to travel. Someone says they flew to Japan and watched the curve out the window. You say, “That’s just optical illusion.” You say, “You only see it because you were told to see it.” More eyes roll. You laugh loudest. Inside, you want them to change their minds. Inside, you hope someone else has a better argument so you can switch sides without losing your place. No one tries. The conversation moves on. You fade out of it. For a moment, you wish everyone believed like you. Then you wish you could believe what everyone else does. Then you don’t know what you want.
content.imageDescription: At a lively teen party, the protagonist stands in a small circle, holding a cup. As someone describes their flight to Japan, the protagonist interrupts with a counterclaim. Others exchange glances and roll their eyes. The protagonist laughs, then is shown fading into the background, looking lost and unsure as the group moves on.

content.text: You start to see yourself the way others do: unsure, reaching for certainty, holding onto whatever sounds most convincing on a given day. But nobody sees that part. They only see the kid who argues the earth is flat. People avoid you after class. Teachers don’t call on you. People stop replying in the group chat. The friends who agreed with you are the ones who never show their faces. You look around and realize you’ve been following voices that sound certain but never say anything new.
content.imageDescription: A reflective shot: the protagonist watches their own faint reflection in a school window, while students in the hall give them distance. In class, teachers avoid calling on them and their phone notifications slow. Even their online messages come only from faceless avatars.

content.text: One night, lying on your bed, you scroll back through your earliest posts. You try to remember when you started believing. You can’t find the moment. It feels like you always have, but you know that’s not true. You remember Jamie’s voice, confident, talking about debate, how to gather evidence. You remember how good it felt to win by sounding sure. Now winning is less sweet. Now you don’t know if it’s even possible. You close your eyes. You imagine standing at the edge of the world, looking out, waiting for someone to tell you which way is east.
content.imageDescription: The protagonist lies alone in bed at night, illuminated by a phone screen, scrolling through their oldest social media posts. Eyes closed, they imagine a surreal landscape at the edge of the world, standing uncertainly and searching the horizon for direction.

content.text: You are a flat earther. You didn’t get there because of science. You got there because the loudest voices said it with confidence. You repeated what you heard because repeating feels like knowing and knowing feels like being safe. You want to believe so badly that you stop noticing whose belief you’re borrowing. You are certain only because certainty is easier than doubt and the world rewards people who pretend not to question. You didn’t test, you didn’t search, you didn’t ask too many questions because someone else already did. Or they said they did. You believed because believing filled the space where answers should be, because fitting in somewhere was easier than standing alone with your hand up waiting for real proof.
content.imageDescription: A thoughtful montage: the protagonist surrounded by a swirl of sound bites, echoing voices, and social media screens. Scenes flash of them copying talking points, being rewarded online, and finally sitting alone, hand raised in an empty classroom, searching for validation.

content.text: If this feels too close, if you see some part of yourself in the need to belong, in the urge to sound certain, in the times you’d rather just pick a side and argue it loud, drop a comment. If you had grown up surrounded by voices that said flat is true, would you have believed differently? Because if the answer is maybe, this story isn’t only about a flat earther—it’s about anyone who ever let someone else’s confidence become their certainty. Subscribe for more. See you next time.
content.imageDescription: A closing image: the protagonist looks directly at the viewer, thoughtful, their face surrounded by comment bubbles and social media icons, inviting connection. A call-to-action banner with 'Subscribe for more' and 'See you next time' is subtly included in the background.
